ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: To Mauricius Augustus.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to Mauricius, &c.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: The Piety of my Lords in their most serene commands, while set on refuting me on certain matters, in sparing me has by no means spared me.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For by the use therein of the term simplicity they politely call me silly.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: It is true indeed that in Holy Scripture, when simplicity is spoken of in a good sense, it is often carefully associated with prudence and uprightness.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Hence it is written of the blessed Job, The man was simple and upright (Job i.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And the blessed Apostle Paul admonishes saying Be ye simple in evil and prudent in good (Rom. xvi.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And the Truth in person admonishes saying, Be ye prudent as serpents, and simple as doves (Matth. x.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 16); thus shewing it to be very unprofitable if either prudence should be wanting to simplicity, or simplicity to prudence.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: In order, then, to make His servants instructed for all things He desired them to be both simple as doves, and prudent as serpents, that so both the cunning of the serpent might sharpen in them the simplicity of the dove, and the simplicity of the dove temper the cunning of the serpent.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: I therefore, who am denounced in the most serene commands of my Lords as simple without the addition of prudence, as having been deceived by the cunning of Ariulph, am plainly and undoubtedly called silly; which I also myself acknowledge to be the case.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, though your Piety were silent, the facts cry out.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, if I had not been silly, I should by no means have come to endure what I suffer in this place among the swords of the Lombards.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Moreover, in what I stated about Ariulph, that he was prepared with all his heart to come to terms with the republic, seeing that I am not believed, I am reproved also as having lied.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, although I am not a priest, I know it to be a grave injury to a priest that, being a servant of the truth, he should be believed to be deceitful.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And I have been for some time aware that Nordulph is believed before me, and Leo before me, and that now easy credence is given to those who seem to be in your confidence more than to my assertions.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And indeed if the captivity of my land were not increasing day by day, I would gladly pass over in silence contempt and ridicule of myself.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But this does afflict me exceedingly, that from my bearing the charge of falsehood it ensues also that Italy is daily led captive under the yoke of the Lombards.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, while my representations are in no wise believed, the strength of the enemy is increasing hugely.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: This however I suggest to my most pious lord, that he would think anything that is bad of me, but, with regard to the advantage of the republic and the cause of the rescue of Italy, not easily lend his pious ears to any one, but believe facts rather than words.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Moreover, let not our lord, in virtue of his earthly power, too hastily disdain priests, but with excellent consideration, on account of Him whose servants they are, so rule over them as also to pay the reverence that is due to them.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For in Holy Writ priests are sometimes called gods, and sometimes angels.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For even through Moses it is said of him who is to be put upon his oath, Bring him unto the gods (Exod. xxii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 8); that is unto the priests.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And again it is written, Thou shalt not revile the gods (Ib.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 28), to wit, the priests.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And the prophet says, The priest’s lips shall keep knowledge, and they shall seek the law at his mouth; for he is the angel of the Lord of hosts (Malach. ii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Why, then, should it be strange if your Piety were to condescend to honour those to whom even God Himself in His word gives honour, calling them angels or gods?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Ecclesiastical history also testifies that, when accusations in writing against bishops had been offered to the Prince Constantine of pious memory, he received indeed the bills of accusation, but, calling together the bishops who had been accused, he burnt before their eyes the bills which he had received, saying, Ye are gods, constituted by the true God.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Go, and settle your causes among you, for it is not fit that we should judge gods.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Yet in this sentence, my pious Lord, he conferred more on himself by his humility than on them by the reverence paid to them.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For before him there were pagan princes in the republic, who knew not the true God, but worshipped gods of wood and stone; and yet they paid the greatest honour to their priests.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: What wonder then if a Christian emperor should condescend to honour the priests of the true God, when pagan princes, as we have already said, knew how to bestow honour on priests who served gods of wood and stone?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: These things, then, I suggest to the piety of my Lords, not in my own behalf, but in behalf of all priests.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For I am a man that is a sinner.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, since I offend against Almighty God incessantly every day, I surmise that it will be some amends for this at the tremendous judgment, that I am smitten incessantly every day by blows.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And I believe that you appease the same Almighty God all the more as you more severely afflict me who serve Him badly.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For I had already received many blows, and when the commands of my Lords came in addition, I found consolations that I was not hoping for.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, if I can, I will briefly enumerate these blows.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: First, that the peace which without any cost to the republic I had made with the Lombards who were in Tuscany was withdrawn from me.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Then, the peace having been broken, the soldiers were removed from the Roman city.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And some indeed were slain by the enemy, but others were placed at Narnii and Perusium (Perugia); and Rome was left, that Perusium might be held.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: After this a still heavier blow was the arrival of Agilulph, so that I saw with my own eyes Romans tied by the neck with ropes like dogs, to be taken to France for sale.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, because we who were within the city under the protection of God escaped his hands, a ground was thence sought for making us appear culpable; to wit, because corn ran short, which cannot by any means be kept in large quantities for long in this city; as I have shewn more fully in another representation.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: On my own account indeed I was in no wise disturbed, since I declare, my conscience bearing me witness, that I was prepared to suffer any adversity whatever, so long as I came out of all these things with the safety of my soul.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But for the glorious men, Gregory the præfect, and Castorius the military commander (magistro militum), I have been distressed in no small degree, seeing that they in no way neglected to do all that could be done, and endured most severe toil in watching and guarding the city during the siege, and, after all this, were smitten by the heavy indignation of my Lords.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: As to them, I clearly understand that it is not their conduct, but my person, that goes against them.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, having with me alike laboured in trouble, they are alike troubled after labour.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Now as to the Piety of my Lords holding out over me the formidable and terrible judgment of Almighty God, I beseech you by the same Almighty God to do this no more.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For as yet we know not how any of us will stand there.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And Paul, the excellent preacher, says, Judge nothing before the time, until the Lord come, who both will bring to light the hidden things of darkness, and will make manifest the counsels of the hearts (1 Cor. iv.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Yet this I briefly say, that, unworthy sinner as I am, I rely more on the mercy of Jesus when He comes than on the justice of your Piety.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And there are many things that men are ignorant of with regard to this judgment; for perhaps He will blame what you praise, and praise what you blame.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Wherefore among all these uncertainties I return to tears only, praying that the same Almighty God may both direct our most pious Lord with His hand and in that terrible judgment find him free from all defaults.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And may He make me so to please men, if need be, as not to offend against His eternal grace.
